title: How the Long-Lost Body of Richard III Was Found Under a Parking Lot: Solving a 500-Year-Old Mystery
description:
in History |   	March 12th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

Shake┬şspeareÔÇÖs The Tragedy of Richard the Third begins with the epony┬şmous char┬şac┬şter utter┬ş
body:
in History |   	March 12th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

Shake┬şspeareÔÇÖs The Tragedy of Richard the Third begins with the epony┬şmous char┬şac┬şter utter┬şing the famous line ÔÇťNow is the win┬şter of our dis┬şcon┬ştent.ÔÇŁ It ends at the Bat┬ştle of Bosworth Field, by which point his vil┬şlain┬şous schemes have come to ruin and his deser┬ştion by Lord Stan┬şley seems to have sealed his fate. ÔÇťA horse, a horse, my king┬şdom for a horse,ÔÇŁ he cries out, coin┬şing anoth┬şer expres┬şsion used four cen┬şturies lat┬şer before being slain by the Earl of Rich┬şmond, the man who would be Hen┬şry VII. Though Shake┬şspeare him┬şself was writ┬şing more than 100 years after the his┬ştor┬şi┬şcal events he dra┬şma┬ştized, he includ┬şed lit┬ştle after the event of RichardÔÇÖs death, whose most fas┬şci┬şnat┬şing mys┬ştery was in any case only solved in our own time.
You can see the sto┬şry of Richard IIIÔÇÖs long-unknown where┬şabouts in the Pri┬şmal Space video above. Accord┬şing to records, says the nar┬şra┬ştor, ÔÇťhe was buried uncer┬şe┬şmo┬şni┬şous┬şly beneath the Greyfri┬şars Church in Leices┬şter, and a mon┬şu┬şment was even┬ştu┬şal┬şly placed above his grave.ÔÇŁ When Hen┬şry VIII ordered such hous┬şes of wor┬şship shut down forty years lat┬şer, Greyfri┬şars was among the insti┬ştu┬ştions demol┬şished.
Every┬şone even┬ştu┬şal┬şly came to believe that, amid this destruc┬ştion, RichardÔÇÖs body had been exhumed and tossed off the Bow Bridge. Only in the ear┬şly two-thou┬şsands did a search for his corpse com┬şmence in earnest, spear┬şhead┬şed by the Richard III Soci┬şety. Hav┬şing deter┬şmined that the Bow Bridge sto┬şry had been made up, the soci┬şetyÔÇÖs mem┬şbers then had to pin down the long-con┬şfused for┬şmer loca┬ştion of Greyfri┬şars Church.
One of them, Philip┬şpa Lan┬şg┬şley, got the hunch to start look┬şing under a Leices┬şter park┬şing lot. Bud┬şgetary lim┬şi┬şta┬ştions forced her team to try dig┬şging just three trench┬şes across spaces like┬şli┬şest to cross the churchÔÇÖs foot┬şprint. ÔÇťAmaz┬şing┬şly, just six hours into the first day, they came across a skele┬ştonÔÇŁ with skull dam┬şage and spinal cur┬şva┬şture. Richard was indeed described as a ÔÇťhunch┬şbackÔÇŁ in his life┬ştime, but in the twen┬şty-first cen┬ştu┬şry, only DNA evi┬şdence clos┬şes the case. Its acqui┬şsi┬ştion neces┬şsi┬ştat┬şed both find┬şing a cou┬şple unbro┬şken female lines (the only means of trans┬şmit┬şting mito┬şchon┬şdr┬şi┬şal DNA) from his sis┬şter down to liv┬şing, testable indi┬şvid┬şu┬şals while car┬şbon-dat┬şing the skele┬şton. Sure enough, Richard turned out to have been in eter┬şnal repose not just under that park┬şing lot, but near a sten┬şciled let┬şter R ÔÇö the kind of coin┬şci┬şdence from which even the Bard him┬şself might have shied away.
Relat┬şed con┬ştent:
Con┬şfirmed: The Bones of Richard III (1452ÔÇô1485) Found Under a UK Park┬şing Lot
74 Ways Char┬şac┬şters Die in ShakespeareÔÇÖs Plays Shown in a Handy Info┬şgraph┬şic: From Snakebites to Lack of Sleep
How Eng┬şland First Became Eng┬şland: An Ani┬şmat┬şed His┬şto┬şry
Hear What Ham┬şlet, Richard III & King Lear Sound┬şed Like in ShakespeareÔÇÖs Orig┬şi┬şnal Pro┬şnun┬şci┬şa┬ştion
A New Analy┬şsis of BeethovenÔÇÖs DNA Reveals That Lead Poi┬şson┬şing Could Have Caused His Deaf┬şness
Based in Seoul,┬áCol┬şin┬áMarshall┬áwrites and broad┬şcasts on cities, lan┬şguage, and cul┬şture. HeÔÇÖs the author of the newslet┬şter┬áBooks on Cities┬áas well as the books┬áÝĽťŕÁş ýÜöýĽŻ ŕŞłýžÇ┬á(No Sum┬şma┬şriz┬şing Korea) and┬áKore┬şan Newtro.┬áFol┬şlow him on the social net┬şwork for┬şmer┬şly known as Twit┬şter at┬á@colinmarshall.

title: Hear Seven Hours of Women Making Electronic Music (1938ÔÇô2014)
description:
Image via Wiki┬şme┬şdia Com┬şmons
A num┬şber of years ago, in a post on the pio┬şneer┬şing com┬şpos┬şer of the orig┬şi┬şnal Doc┬ştor Who theme, we wrote tha
body:
Image via Wiki┬şme┬şdia Com┬şmons
A num┬şber of years ago, in a post on the pio┬şneer┬şing com┬şpos┬şer of the orig┬şi┬şnal Doc┬ştor Who theme, we wrote that ÔÇťthe ear┬şly era of exper┬şi┬şmen┬ştal elec┬ştron┬şic music belonged to Delia Der┬şbyshire.ÔÇŁ DerbyshireÔÇöwho almost gave Paul McCart┬şney a ver┬şsion of ÔÇťYes┬şter┬şdayÔÇŁ with an elec┬ştron┬şic back┬şing in place of stringsÔÇöhelped invent the ear┬şly elec┬ştron┬şic music of the six┬şties through her work with the Radio┬şphon┬şic Work┬şshop, the sound effects lab┬şo┬şra┬şto┬şry of the BBC. She went on to form one of the most influ┬şen┬ştial, if large┬şly obscure, elec┬ştron┬şic acts of the decade, White Noise. And yet, call┬şing the ear┬şly eras of elec┬ştron┬şic music hers is an exag┬şger┬şa┬ştion. Of course her many col┬şlab┬şo┬şra┬ştors deserve men┬ştion, as well as musi┬şcians like Bruce Haack, Pierre Hen┬şry, Kraftwerk, Bri┬şan Eno, and so many oth┬şers. But what gets almost com┬şplete┬şly left out of many his┬şto┬şries of elec┬ştron┬şic music, as with so many oth┬şer his┬şto┬şries, is the promi┬şnent role so many women besides Der┬şbyshire played in the devel┬şop┬şment of the sounds we now hear around us all the time.
In recog┬şni┬ştion of this fact, musi┬şcian, DJ, and ÔÇťescaped housewife/schoolteacherÔÇŁ Bar┬şbara Gold┬şen devot┬şed two episodes┬áof her KPFA radio pro┬şgram ÔÇťCrack oÔÇÖ DawnÔÇŁ to women in elec┬ştron┬şic music, once in 2010 and again in 2013. She shares each broad┬şcast with co-host Jon Lei┬şdeck┬şer (ÔÇťWob┬şblyÔÇŁ), and in each seg┬şment, the two ban┬şter in casu┬şal radio show style, offer┬şing his┬şto┬şry and con┬ştext for each musi┬şcian and com┬şpos┬şer. High┬şlight┬şed on UbuÔÇÖs for┬şmer Twit┬şter stream, the first show, ÔÇťWomen in Elec┬ştron┬şic Music 1938ÔÇô1982 Part 1ÔÇŁ (above) gives Der┬şbyshire her due, with three tracks from her, includ┬şing the Doc┬ştor Who theme.
It also includes music from twen┬şty one┬áoth┬şer com┬şposers, begin┬şning with Clara Rock┬şmore, a refin┬şer and pop┬şu┬şlar┬şiz┬şer of the theremin, that weird instru┬şment designed to sim┬şu┬şlate a high, tremu┬şlous human voice. Also fea┬ştured is Wendy CarlosÔÇÖs ÔÇťTimesteps,ÔÇŁ an orig┬şi┬şnal piece from her A Clock┬şwork Orange score.
The sec┬şond show, above, fills in sev┬şer┬şal gaps in the orig┬şi┬şnal broad┬şcast and ÔÇťcould eas┬şi┬şly be six hoursÔÇŁ says co-host Lei┬şdeck┬şer, giv┬şen the sheer amount of elec┬ştron┬şic music out there com┬şposed and record┬şed by women over the past sev┬şen┬şty┬áyears. This show includes one of our host GoldenÔÇÖs own com┬şpo┬şsi┬ştions, ÔÇťMelody Sum┬şn┬şer Car┬şna┬şhan,ÔÇŁ as well as music from Lau┬şrie Ander┬şson and musique con┬şcrete com┬şpos┬şer Doris Hays. These two broad┬şcasts alone cov┬şer an enor┬şmous range of styl┬şis┬ştic and tech┬şno┬şlog┬şi┬şcal ground, but for even more disco┬şgraph┬şi┬şcal his┬şto┬şry of women in elec┬ştron┬şic music, see the playlist below, com┬şpiled by ÔÇťNerd┬şgirlÔÇŁ Antye Greie-Ripat┬şti. Com┬şmis┬şsioned by Club Trans┬şme┬şdi┬şale Berlin, the mix includes such well-known names as Yoko Ono, Bjork, and M.I.A., as well as fore┬şmoth┬şers Der┬şbyshire and Car┬şlos, and dozens more.
In lieu of the radio-show chat┬şter of Gold┬şen and Lei┬şdeck┬şer, we have Greie-RipattiÔÇÖs post detail┬şing each artistÔÇÖs time peri┬şod, coun┬ştry of ori┬şgin, and con┬ştri┬şbu┬ştions to elec┬ştron┬şic music his┬şto┬şry. Many of the com┬şposers rep┬şre┬şsent┬şed here worked for major radio and film stu┬şdios, scored fea┬şture films (like 1956ÔÇÖs For┬şbid┬şden Plan┬şet), invent┬şed and inno┬şvat┬şed new instru┬şments and tech┬şniques, wrote for orches┬ştras, and passed on their knowl┬şedge as edu┬şca┬ştors and pro┬şduc┬şers. Greie-RipattiÔÇÖs page quotes a Dan┬şish elec┬ştron┬şic pro┬şduc┬şer and per┬şformer say┬şing ÔÇťthere is a lot of women in elec┬ştron┬şic musicÔÇŽ invis┬şi┬şble women.ÔÇŁ Thanks to efforts like hers and GoldenÔÇÖs, these pio┬şneer┬şing cre┬şators need no longer go unseen or, more impor┬ştant┬şly, unheard.
Note: An ear┬şli┬şer ver┬şsion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent:
Meet Delia Der┬şbyshire, the┬áDr. Who┬áCom┬şpos┬şer Who Almost Turned The Bea┬şt┬şlesÔÇÖ ÔÇťYes┬şter┬şdayÔÇŁ Into Ear┬şly Elec┬ştron┬şi┬şca
Watch Com┬şpos┬şer Wendy Car┬şlos Demo an Orig┬şi┬şnal Moog Syn┬şthe┬şsiz┬şer (1989)
Meet Four Women Who Pio┬şneered Elec┬ştron┬şic Music: Daphne Oram, Lau┬şrie Spiegel, ├ëliane Radigue & Pauline Oliv┬şeros
New Doc┬şu┬şmen┬ştary┬áSis┬şters with Tran┬şsis┬ştors┬áTells the Sto┬şry of Elec┬ştron┬şic MusicÔÇÖs Female Pio┬şneers
Mr. Rogers Intro┬şduces Kids to Exper┬şi┬şmen┬ştal Elec┬ştron┬şic Music by Bruce Haack & Esther Nel┬şson (1968)
Hear┬áElec┬ştron┬şic Lady┬şland, a Mix┬ştape Fea┬ştur┬şing 55 Tracks from 35 Pio┬şneer┬şing Women in Elec┬ştron┬şic Music
Thomas Dol┬şby Explains How a Syn┬şthe┬şsiz┬şer Works on a Jim Hen┬şson Kids Show (1989)
Josh Jones is a writer and musi┬şcian based in Durham, NC.┬á

title: The Monty Python Philosophy Soccer Match: The Ancient Greeks Versus the Germans
description:
in Comedy, Sports |   	March 13th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

Today, weÔÇÖre revis┬şit┬şing a clas┬şsic Mon┬şty Python skit. The scene is the 1972 Munich
body:
in Comedy, Sports |   	March 13th, 2026  2 Comments
	
			

	

Today, weÔÇÖre revis┬şit┬şing a clas┬şsic Mon┬şty Python skit. The scene is the 1972 Munich Olympics. The event is a football/soccer match, pit┬şting Ger┬şman philoso┬şphers against Greek philoso┬şphers. On the one side, the Ger┬şmans ÔÇö Hegel, Niet┬şzsche, Kant, Marx and, um, Franz Beck┬şen┬şbauer. On the oth┬şer side, Archimedes, Socrates, Pla┬şto and the rest of the gang. The ref┬şer┬şee? Con┬şfu┬şcius. Of course.
Enjoy!
If you would like to sign up for Open CultureÔÇÖs free email newslet┬şter,┬áplease find it here. ItÔÇÖs a great way to see our new posts, all bun┬şdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to sup┬şport the mis┬şsion of Open Cul┬şture, con┬şsid┬şer mak┬şing┬áa dona┬ştion to our site. ItÔÇÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬ştri┬şbu┬ştions will help us con┬ştin┬şue pro┬şvid┬şing the best free cul┬ştur┬şal and edu┬şca┬ştion┬şal mate┬şri┬şals to learn┬şers every┬şwhere. You can con┬ştribute through Pay┬şPal, Patre┬şon, and Ven┬şmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬şed Con┬ştent:
Why Jorge Luis Borges Hat┬şed Soc┬şcer: ÔÇťSoc┬şcer is Pop┬şu┬şlar Because Stu┬şpid┬şi┬şty is Pop┬şu┬şlarÔÇŁ

title: Longevity and Obsoletion Impress Upon Alexander Endrullat's Intaglio Prints
description: Leipzig, Germany-based artist Alexander Endrullat has traded traditional Intaglio printing plates for discarded laptops. His ongoing series titled
body:
Leipzig, Germany-based artist Alexander Endrullat has traded traditional Intaglio printing plates for discarded laptops. His ongoing series titled Off the Grid emerged from a familiar yet annoying scenario: owning an older device that can no longer be updated, rendering it practically unusable. EndrullatÔÇÖs frustration led him to a moment of impulsivity as he pushed his device through a printing press, coincidentally discovering the distinctive technique.
ÔÇťOne of the most interesting aspects of the process is how clearly the progressive destruction of the devices becomes visible after each print,ÔÇŁ the artist explains. With each pass through the device becomes increasingly altered, revealing details about its internal structures and their previous ownersÔÇöworn keys that must have been used the most, traces of sticker residue, or remnants of webcam covers. Wiping off ink in between prints also becomes more challenging throughout the process, as glass screens and touchpads begin to crack.

The artistÔÇÖs studio printing press is just about one hundred years old, introducing a fascinating contrast between the longevity of toolsÔÇöboth robust and enduring or short-lived, perhaps even reminiscent of planned obsolescence. ÔÇťThe series reflects on materiality, consumption, and the hidden architectures ofdigital devices,ÔÇŁ he says. ÔÇťAlthough I might also simply enjoy the sound a laptop makes the first time it runs through the press.ÔÇŁ
Endrullat hopes to experiment with printing entire computer setups, complete with mice, keyboards, monitors, and more, which would eventually call for a different type of press. Take a peek at the artistÔÇÖs process and find more work on his Instagram.

schema.headline: Longevity and Obsoletion Impress Upon Alexander Endrullat's Intaglio Prints

title: A Visit to Tom├ís Saraceno's Berlin Studio Delves into a Deeply Empathetic Practice
description: What is a web to the spider? A home, a tool, simply something they cling to? Tom├ís Saraceno presents these questions in a new segment from Art21,
body:
What is a web to the spider? A home, a tool, simply something they cling to? Tom├ís Saraceno presents these questions in a new segment from Art21, in which filmmakers visit his Berlin studio and examine the machinations of his collaborative practice, extending from a team of people to the tiny critters beneath our feet.
Saraceno continually considers how humans occupy space and how such environments inform the ways we connect with the world around us. This short documentary, which is part of the ÔÇťRealms of the RealÔÇŁ episode, reviews several of the artistÔÇÖs projects, from his suspended installations to his more participatory community projects.



Several artworks presented in the film have been previously featured on Colossal, and the film offers insight into the evolution of SaracenoÔÇÖs thinking over several years. Much of his work strives for connection and empathy building, which he explains through the structure of the web. ÔÇťItÔÇÖs really trying to extend the ability of understanding who is our family, right? Who is our brothers, sisters, and grandfathers?ÔÇŁ he says. ÔÇťBy allowing others to admire these incredible webs, they will become more empathetic.ÔÇŁ
Find more Art21 films on YouTube, along with some of our favorites previously on Colossal.

schema.headline: A Visit to Tom├ís Saraceno's Berlin Studio Delves into a Deeply Empathetic Practice

title: In 'Life Forms,' Janny Baek Imagines a Speculative Landscape
description:
Joy Machine is pleased to present┬áLife Forms, a solo exhibition by Janny Baek, on view from March 20 to May 9, 2026.
How do we conceive of change?
body:
Joy Machine is pleased to present┬áLife Forms, a solo exhibition by Janny Baek, on view from March 20 to May 9, 2026.
How do we conceive of change? With fear, excitement, or uncertainty? As Janny Baek builds sculptural ceramics of speculative beings and imagined landscapes, she grapples with these questions. The work follows its own dream logic, one that accepts incongruity and dissonance as necessary to play and experimentation. Marbling hunks of colored clay, coiling bases, and molding a singular material into something new is part of an exploratory practice that embraces transformation and its often strange outcomes.
Detail of ÔÇťDream StateÔÇŁ (2024)
Life Forms┬áemerges from this dual meaning, invoking both the act of creation and the fantastical works it produces. Neither wholly abstract nor representational, BaekÔÇÖs sculptures draw on natural structures and processes and invite us to question how we interpret the world around us. Recognizable forms like open blossoms, birds, and creatures are met with the unexpected. These lively components make even the more abstract works appear animate, like ambiguous organisms that might decide to scuttle away. They evoke something primordial and yet are exhilaratingly new.
Baek paints in the way she sketches, as a means of developing ideas and visualizing their potential. For her ceramics, she incorporates hand building alongside the Japanese pottery technique known as┬ánerikomi, which involves splicing and designing patterns with strips of colored clay. ÔÇťMy material choices are a way of thinking about natural processes: color gradients as the continuous nature of change, a multitude of colors as potential, abundance, and vitality, and patterns as signals and communications,ÔÇŁ she says.
Hovering between worlds, BaekÔÇÖs work populates a speculative environment in which beings morph, mutate, and blossom, their individual features forming an otherworldly lineage thatÔÇÖs recognizable but not identical. While ÔÇťtemporarily and imperfectly captured in a moment of many possible transformations,ÔÇŁ the works beckon us into a world in which change is not only inevitable but also the most alluring proposition.
Life Forms┬áis BaekÔÇÖs Chicago debut. An opening reception will be held on March 20, and the artist will be present.┬áSee more from Baek previously on Colossal.
ÔÇťFuture BlossomsÔÇŁ (2023), colored porcelain, 13 x 13 x 12 inches
ÔÇťLandscapeÔÇŁ (2025), colored stoneware with glazed interior, 15 x 8 x 8 inches
Detail of ÔÇťDream StateÔÇŁ (2024)
ÔÇťDream State 2ÔÇŁ (2025), stoneware, colored slip, porcelain, and glaze, 21 x 17 x 16 inches
Detail of ÔÇťLandscapeÔÇŁ (2025)
ÔÇťPlant LifeÔÇŁ (2025), stoneware, colored stoneware, and glaze, 21.5 x 15 x 15 inches
Detail of ÔÇťPlant LifeÔÇŁ (2025)

title: You'll Need a Magnifying Glass to Read Some of the World's Smallest Books at the V&A
description: At Windsor Castle, a one-of-a-kind architectural marvel isnÔÇÖt a structural part of the building itself or even a full-size feature. Here, youÔÇÖll f
body:
At Windsor Castle, a one-of-a-kind architectural marvel isnÔÇÖt a structural part of the building itself or even a full-size feature. Here, youÔÇÖll find Queen MaryÔÇÖs DollsÔÇÖ House, widely regarded as the largest and most famous in the world. Designed by architect Sir Edwin Lutyens, the house was built between 1921 and 1924 and contains items and furnishings conceived of by hundreds of the leading craftspeople and artisans of the day.
Queen Mary, consort to King George V between 1910 and 1936, was an enthusiast of all things miniature. Her dollsÔÇÖ house even contains scale versions of nearly 600 real books in its library, including works by literary giants like A.A. Milne and Sir Arthur Conan Doyle. Commissioned from publishers around the U.K. and farther afield, many of these books are also held in the collections of the V&A, where they have been on long-term loan since 1916.



A recent video produced by the museum glimpses some of these tiny treasures with the expert guidance of National Art Library Assistant Librarian Amy McMullan and Catherine Yvard, Curator of National Art Library Collections. Examples include a red leather-bound Bible published in 1896 by Glasgow-based David Bryce & Son, in addition to a Quran, a collection of poems by Robert Burns, and more.
The National Art Library is housed within the V&A, and more than a million publications related to art, design, and performance comprise an archive that spans the 8th century to today.
Many more miniature books comprise the museumÔÇÖs holdings, in addition to Queen MaryÔÇÖs collection. Little almanacs in their embellished folios were published annually and included notable dates, such as sunrises and sunset times, holidays, and other practical information. Many of the titles sport gilt edges, marbled papers, and even metal cases that double as lockets so that they could be worn.
The collection includes diminutive dictionaries, a souvenir of The Great Exhibition of 1851 in Hyde Park, and childrenÔÇÖs booksÔÇöincluding a number of tunnel books, or paper peep-shows. These accordion-style tomes look at first glance like any other publication, but they expand into long tunnels through which viewers can take in a layered, dimensional scene.

The oldest object in the V&AÔÇÖs collection is an early 1700s silver-bound miniature prayerbook in French thatÔÇÖs embellished with the semi-precious stone lapis lazuli. And the tiniest is fittingly called The Smallest Book in the World, published in 2002 and measuring 2.4 by 2.9 millimeters. It was designed by a German typographer and is so tiny that itÔÇÖs accompanied by a small pamphlet showing what you would see if you could page through the book. As McMullen explains, the physical size of the book begs an interesting question: ÔÇťIs it really a book if you canÔÇÖt read it?ÔÇŁ
Visitors are welcome to peruse the online catalogue and interact with items in the collection in person in the V&A East StorehouseÔÇÖs reading rooms. Explore more on the museumÔÇÖs YouTube channel.

schema.headline: You'll Need a Magnifying Glass to Read Some of the World's Smallest Books at the V&A
